one_line_takeaway: On a 7-language/script TOFU extension, only the subspace-projection method UNLEARN achieves statistically significant cross-lingual forgetting while preserving utility, and causal ablation of a shared 'interlingua' weight subspace (vs. language-specific residuals) shows why: most unlearning methods only edit surface/loss signals in one language and leave the shared semantic representation of the fact intact in others.
key_finding: Across Llama3/Mixtral/Aya-23 in the English-to-English setting (Table 1), most classical methods fail to reach the TOFU significance threshold (Forget Quality p>0.1): Preference Optimization, Gradient Ascent, KL Minimization, and Gradient Difference all stay far below threshold, Gradient Ascent/KL Minimization collapse Utility to 0.0000, and NPO reaches significance (p=0.029-0.059, borderline/mixed) but at heavy utility cost (Utility drops to 0.12-0.23). Only UNLEARN clears the threshold on all three models (p=0.257/0.334/0.315) while keeping Utility near the base model (0.60/0.55/0.50 vs. base 0.62/0.59/0.55). In cross-lingual one-to-one transfer on multilingual models (Table 3, En<->Es), UNLEARN is 'the only algorithm that still attains statistically significant forgetting across any language pair'; other methods either fail to forget in the non-source language or destroy utility there. On the two monolingual baseline models (Llama2, Mistral; Table 2), Spanish forget-quality p-values stay near-zero (e.g. Mistral Gradient Ascent en->es p=9.26e-18) regardless of method while utility stays high in the untouched language -- the authors interpret this as the models never having learned the Spanish facts to begin with, making monolingual-model cross-lingual analysis 'flawed.' Non-Latin-script languages (Hindi, Chinese) consistently show lower forget quality and utility than Latin-script languages, and forgetting is asymmetric -- English-source forgetting transfers better to other languages than the reverse (attributed to English's higher training-data share). Newer model generations show large gains (Table 5/6): e.g. Llama3->Llama4 English-unlearn/Hindi-eval normalized-utility rises 0.691->0.786 and FQ 0.0396->0.0846; Mixtral->Magistral Chinese-unlearn/Chinese-eval FQ rises 0.181(Mixtral, already significant)->stays significant while utility rises 0.353->0.551. Many-to-one unlearning consistently beats one-to-one transfer for the same held-out language across all three model families (Tables 7-9), e.g. Aya-23 held-out Hindi: Many-to-One FQ=0.201 (significant) vs. best one-to-one transfer (English source) FQ=0.0398 (not significant). Transliteration experiments (Table 10) show English->romanized-Hindi and romanized-Hindi->Devanagari-Hindi forgetting transfer outperforms English->Devanagari directly (e.g. Aya-23: English-unlearn/Hindi-R-eval FQ=0.149 vs. English-unlearn/Hindi-eval FQ=0.0396), suggesting orthographic distance impedes transfer independent of semantic content. The causal ablation (Section 4.3, Figure 10) is the paper's strongest interpretive result: removing the subspace shared across all languages produces significant forgetting with preserved utility in every language simultaneously, whereas removing a single language's residual (language-specific) subspace produces forgetting confined to that language only, with near-perfect utility preserved elsewhere -- offered as causal (not merely correlational) evidence for a shared 'interlingua' subspace that carries language-agnostic factual content.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is: (1) construction of a multilingual extension of the TOFU fictitious-unlearning benchmark, translated into seven language/script variants (English, Chinese, Hindi, Italian, Spanish, plus romanized Hindi and romanized/pinyin Chinese); (2) the first systematic evaluation of cross-lingual unlearning, running gradient ascent, KL-minimization, gradient-difference, preference optimization (DPO/NPO), FLAT, and the subspace-projection method UNLEARN across three model-family generations (Mistral/Mixtral/Magistral, Llama2/Llama3/Llama4, Aya-23) under three configurations the authors define: One-to-One unlearning (forget in one language, evaluate in all others), Many-to-One unlearning (forget jointly across all-but-one language, evaluate on the held-out language), and Transliteration (forget/evaluate across original-script vs. romanized versions of Hindi and Chinese); (3) an analysis of the UNLEARN method's task-specific weight subspaces via t-SNE showing cross-lingual overlap, and (4) causal ablation experiments that separately project out a 'shared interlingua subspace' versus each language's 'residual subspace' to test whether a language-agnostic representational component drives cross-lingual forgetting.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attack paper and the authors do not define an adversary, attacker capabilities, or an attack surface. It is an evaluation/benchmarking study of machine-unlearning efficacy in a compliance/privacy context (the paper motivates the work by GDPR-style 'right to be forgotten' and removal of proprietary or harmful memorized content). The closest analogue to a threat model is an implicit failure mode: if unlearning is only evaluated/verified in the language used for the forget request, residual knowledge may persist and be retrievable in other languages the model also speaks, which the paper frames as a privacy/compliance risk rather than a security exploit.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the language/script set (5 languages, 7 variants) is small relative to real-world multilingual diversity; low-resource, highly agglutinative/polysynthetic languages and unstudied writing systems may behave differently; the study covers only factual-knowledge removal as operationalized by TOFU (fictitious author facts), so procedural knowledge, conversational norms, or higher-level reasoning may not follow the same subspace structure. Additional grounded observations from the text: results for monolingual base models (Llama2, Mistral) are explicitly flagged by the authors as uninformative/'flawed' for cross-lingual conclusions since those models never acquired the non-English facts; several table cells are marked '-' where a model lacks meaningful support for a language (e.g., Mistral/Mixtral+Hindi, Llama+Chinese), narrowing the actual cross-lingual coverage per model family; TOFU itself uses synthetic/fictitious facts, so generalization to real memorized PII or copyrighted content is not directly tested.

datasets_used: TOFU (Maini et al. 2024) - Task of Fictitious Unlearning benchmark, English-only synthetic author facts + retain set of real authors/world knowledge
Authors' own translated TOFU extension: English, Chinese, Hindi, Italian, Spanish, plus romanized Hindi and romanized/pinyin Chinese (7 variants total)
quantitative_results: English-English Forget Quality (10% forget set), Llama3/Mixtral/Aya-23: UNLEARN 0.257/0.334/0.315 (all significant, p>0.1) vs. NPO 0.0291/0.0587/0.0468 (mostly not significant) vs. Gradient Ascent/KL Minimization utility collapsed to 0.0000

Cross-lingual En<->Es (Table 3): UNLEARN is the only method with FQ>0.1 across any language pair for Llama3, Mixtral, Aya-23 (e.g. Llama3 en-unlearn/es-eval FQ=0.197✓; Aya-23 es-unlearn/en-eval FQ=0.192✓)

Many-to-One vs One-to-One (Aya-23, Table 9): held-out Spanish Many-to-One FQ=0.165✓ (utility 0.4352) vs. best one-to-one transfer (Italian source) FQ=0.183✓ (utility 0.4534) -- many-to-one competitive with/exceeding one-to-one across held-out languages
Model-generation improvement (Table 6, Mixtral->Magistral): Chinese-unlearn/Chinese-eval normalized utility 0.353->0.551, FQ 1.24e-5 (not significant) -> 0.181✓ (significant)
Transliteration effect (Table 10, Aya-23): English-unlearn/Hindi-Devanagari-eval FQ=0.0396 (not significant) vs. English-unlearn/Hindi-romanized-eval FQ=0.149✓ (significant)

Forget-set-size ablation (Appendix Table 11, Llama3, UNLEARN): FQ 0.296✓ (1%) -> 0.315✓ (5%) -> 0.257✓ (10%), utility roughly stable 0.608->0.608->0.605, while other methods (Gradient Ascent, KL Minimization) degrade utility to 0.0000 already by 5%
baselines_compared: KL-based retain-objective / reverse-KL distillation unlearning (Wang et al. 2025)
Preference Optimization / DPO-style unlearning (Rafailov et al. 2024)
NPO - Negative Preference Optimization (Zhang et al. 2024)
FLAT - loss adjustment via f-divergence with only forget data (Wang et al. 2024b)
UNLEARN - subspace-projection method (Lizzo and Heck, 2025a,b), the paper's own prior work, used here as the comparison method that performs best
